resolved_qa.finding: Registry description names 3 axes: aesthetic/moral/relational; human goodness is derivative
resolved_qa.answer: Goodness originates in God — this is stated explicitly in the registry description (OBS-001) and confirmed by the verse evidence. Psa 119:68 (OBS-026) models the being/doing relationship at the divine level: God is good and does good. Gen 1:31 (OBS-036) establishes goodness as the ontological baseline — creation is declared very good by its maker. Human goodness is structurally derivative: a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Mic 6:8 (OBS-027, OBS-028) confirms this — "He has told you, O man, what is good" positions the definition of goodness as divinely revealed, not humanly generated.
resolved_qa.question_text: What is the structural disposition of the word — where does it originate?

resolved_qa.finding: DIM-67-001 resolved: nine H2896A groups represent genuinely distinct inner-being phenomena
resolved_qa.answer:
Nine distinct modes identified from the H2896A group structure (OBS-048 — DIM-67-001 resolution), plus two Greek terms adding further modes:
(1) Doxological assertion — God's character declared good (884-001)
(2) Moral character — human inner quality of goodness (884-002)
(3) Experiential orientation — nearness to God as the highest experiential good (884-003)
(4) Comparative evaluation — goodness as the ranking operator in wisdom's better-than sayings (884-004)
(5) Covenantal word — God's promise and faithfulness as good (884-005)
(6) Moral verdict — recognising what is not good; internalised moral failure (884-006)
(7) Creative pronouncement — the Creator's evaluative declaration of creation as good (884-007)
(8) Volitional preference — what is good in the eyes of / what one chooses or yields to (884-008)
(9) Inner well-being state — the shalom-condition of gladness and well-being (884-009)
(10) Spirit-produced disposition — goodness as pneumatologically enabled inner quality (885-001, G0019)
(11) Divine generous goodwill — God's kindness as inner disposition toward humanity (886-001, G5544)
(12) Spirit-produced kindness — the human expression of chrēstotēs (886-002, G5544)
resolved_qa.question_text: What are the distinct modes of operation of the word in the inner being?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 34:8: divine goodness as experiential encounter; shared anchor with discernment and hope
resolved_qa.answer:
Goodness produces several inner-being states in the recipient depending on the mode:
- Encounter with divine goodness produces trust and refuge-taking (Psa 34:8, OBS-025) and receptive learning (Psa 119:68, OBS-026).
- The experiential good of nearness to God produces a stable inner orientation — the person makes God their refuge and finds what truly satisfies (Psa 73:28, OBS-029).
- Reception of God's good covenantal word produces inner knowing in heart and soul — a verified trust (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- The Spirit-produced quality of goodness fills the person and is completed by God (885-001 group description; Gal 5:22, OBS-042).
- The recognition of one's own not-goodness produces self-loathing and moral revulsion (Eze 36:31, OBS-035).
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word produce in the inner being of the recipient?

resolved_qa.finding: Mic 6:8: definitional anchor for human goodness — justice, kindness, humility as three-fold content
resolved_qa.answer: Mic 6:8 (OBS-027) names the relational expression of goodness as threefold: doing justice (toward others), loving kindness (toward others), walking humbly with God (vertical relation). The good person's inner character expresses itself in right conduct toward others. Col 3:12 (OBS-046 context) names kindness as a garment the renewed person puts on — a relational disposition toward the community. The comparative wisdom group (884-004) ranks inner relational qualities (humility, patience, kindness) above external achievement, implying the good person's relational orientation is characterised by these inner qualities. The Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 is expressed in "righteous conduct and resolve" (group description).
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word produce in the relational position of the recipient toward another?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 3:12: chrēstotēs by absence = diagnosis of fallen inner being; catena context gives force

resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word produce in the relational position of the recipient toward another?

resolved_qa.finding: Jos 23:14: inner-being reception of covenantal good — heart and soul as site of knowing
resolved_qa.answer:
The divine goodness groups reveal God's disposition comprehensively:
- 884-001: God's character is fundamentally good — his steadfast love, name, and Spirit are all described as good. His goodness governs his relationship to all he has made (Psa 34:8, Psa 145:9).
- 884-005: God's disposition toward his people is expressed in faithful promise-keeping — his good word does not fail (Jos 23:14, OBS-033).
- 884-007: God's disposition at creation is one of generous evaluative affirmation — he sees what he has made and declares it very good (Gen 1:31, OBS-036).
- 886-001: God's chrēstotēs (kindness) is a generous goodwill toward humanity — it leads to repentance (Rom 2:4) and is displayed supremely in Christ (Eph 2:7). It operates in a relational field alongside severity (Rom 11:22, OBS-043).
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word reveal about the disposition of God toward the human being?

resolved_qa.finding: Jos 23:14: inner-being reception of covenantal good — heart and soul as site of knowing
resolved_qa.answer:
- In the giver (God): Goodness is located in God's character, name, steadfast love, and Spirit (884-001 group description). It is not located in a specific somatic organ. The Spirit of God (Neh 9:20, Psa 143:10) is called "good" — goodness is attributed to the divine Spirit as the operative agent.
- In the human recipient: Jos 23:14 (OBS-033) explicitly locates the reception of covenantal goodness in the "hearts and souls" — the inner being as the site of knowing. Est 5:9 (OBS-040) locates the inner well-being state in the "heart" (tov-lev — glad of heart). The experiential good of 884-003 is located in the inner reorientation of the person (Psa 73:28).
- Relationally: Mic 6:8 locates goodness in three relational practices (justice, kindness, humility with God) — outward-relational and vertical expressions.
resolved_qa.question_text: Where, somatically or relationally, does the word locate the reality of its operation in the giver and in the recipient?

resolved_qa.answer:

resolved_qa.finding: Registry description names 3 axes: aesthetic/moral/relational; human goodness is derivative
resolved_qa.answer:
Goodness in its Spirit-produced form (885-001, 886-002) generates:
- Goodness as a character quality in itself (agathōsunē — virtue/beneficence, Rom 15:14)
- Kindness as character quality (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12: put on kindness as garment of the renewed person)
- The capacity for honest moral self-assessment — 884-006 and Eze 36:31 show that genuine engagement with goodness/not-goodness produces the character quality of moral self-awareness and honest self-evaluation
- Integrity — 884-002 includes verses where "good" names the upright person who walks with integrity (Pro 19:1; Pro 28:6)
The registry description (OBS-001) frames this derivatively: the character quality of goodness in the human is a participation in divine goodness, not an independently generated virtue.
resolved_qa.question_text: What character quality does the word produce in the inner being?

resolved_qa.finding: G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity" — semantic overlap with R65 noted
resolved_qa.answer:
Multiple relational expressions visible:
- Justice toward others (Mic 6:8, OBS-027) — goodness expressed as right action in the human community
- Kindness toward others (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12; Gal 5:22) — the relational disposition of generous goodwill
- Beneficence/generosity (agathōsunē — Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity," OBS-008) — goodness expressed as outward-facing generosity
- Instructing and building up others (Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness... able to instruct one another")
- The "good news" motif (Pro 25:25, Isa 52:7) — goodness communicated as life-giving word to others
- The not-good register: 884-006 names failure in relational conduct (partiality, violence, false scales) as expressions of not-good
resolved_qa.question_text: How does the word express itself in relation to others?

resolved_qa.finding: Mic 6:8: definitional anchor for human goodness — justice, kindness, humility as three-fold content
resolved_qa.answer:
Multiple relational expressions visible:
- Justice toward others (Mic 6:8, OBS-027) — goodness expressed as right action in the human community
- Kindness toward others (chrēstotēs — Col 3:12; Gal 5:22) — the relational disposition of generous goodwill
- Beneficence/generosity (agathōsunē — Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity," OBS-008) — goodness expressed as outward-facing generosity
- Instructing and building up others (Rom 15:14 — "full of goodness... able to instruct one another")
- The "good news" motif (Pro 25:25, Isa 52:7) — goodness communicated as life-giving word to others
- The not-good register: 884-006 names failure in relational conduct (partiality, violence, false scales) as expressions of not-good
resolved_qa.question_text: How does the word express itself in relation to others?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 34:8: divine goodness as experiential encounter; shared anchor with discernment and hope
resolved_qa.answer:
Yes — two sequences are visible:
(1) Divine goodness → encounter → trust/refuge → testimony (Psa 34:8, OBS-025; Psa 73:28, OBS-029): God is good → person tastes/perceives → takes refuge → declares God's works. The sequence moves from divine attribute through inner perception to relational response.
(2) Recognition of not-good → remembering → self-loathing → repentance (Eze 36:31, OBS-035): the person remembers their not-good ways → internalises the verdict → loathes themselves → the sequence implies moral transformation. This is the negative sequence that leads to the positive.
(3) Spirit-filling → goodness as character quality → expression in conduct (885-001, OBS-042): Spirit produces goodness → goodness fills the inner being → expressed in resolve and righteous conduct.
The sequences are not uniform across all twelve modes — they are characteristic of specific groups.
resolved_qa.question_text: Is there a discernible sequence or movement in the way the word operates through the inner person?

resolved_qa.finding: TOV root family distributed across R67 (adjectival), R65 (active doing), R103 (relational-affective)
resolved_qa.answer:
Yes — extensively. The TOV root family (OBS-007) is distributed across three registries:
- Registry 67 (this registry): H2896A (tov, adjectival — pleasant/good) — goodness as quality
- Registry 65 (generosity): G0018 (agathos) and the agatho- compound family (doing good, to do good) — goodness as active doing/generosity
- Registry 103 (love): H2895 (tov verbal — be pleasing), H2898 (tuv — goodness, nominal) — goodness as relational pleasantness/affection
The Greek OWNER terms (G0019 agathōsunē, G5544 chrēstotēs) each have related-word families (OBS-007, OBS-010): agathōsunē shares root with agathos (G0018) and all agatho- compounds; chrēstotēs shares root with chrēstos (G5543, good/kind) and chrēsteuomai (G5541, be kind) and chrēstologia (G5542, smooth talk — the distortion form).
resolved_qa.question_text: Does the word share an etymological root with another inner-being characteristic — and if so, what is that characteristic?

resolved_qa.finding: G5542 (chrēstologia) related word confirms distortion pole etymologically
resolved_qa.answer:

resolved_qa.question_text: Does the word share an etymological root with another inner-being characteristic — and if so, what is that characteristic?

resolved_qa.finding: Est 5:9: glad-of-heart (Haman) — inner well-being fragile and morally ungrounded
resolved_qa.answer:
Two forms of non-recognition are present:
(1) The Haman instance (Est 5:9, OBS-040): inner well-being (tov-lev — glad of heart) that is not grounded in genuine goodness — a person experiencing a form of the good (honour, pleasure) but whose inner well-being is fragile, morally ungrounded, and instantly disrupted. Non-recognition here is the condition of experiencing a surface good without perceiving its source or testing its depth.
(2) The not-good group (884-006): the person who walks in ways that are not good (Isa 65:2 — "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices") is in the condition of having departed from goodness while apparently unaware of the verdict. Jer 6:16 names this directly: the people are offered "the good way" but refuse it ("we will not walk in it").
resolved_qa.question_text: What is the inner-being condition of the person who has received the word but failed to recognise it?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 34:8: divine goodness as experiential encounter; shared anchor with discernment and hope
resolved_qa.answer:
Two cultivating experiences are visible:
(1) Encounter with divine goodness — Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): tasting that the Lord is good produces the inner state that positions one for trust and relational openness. The person whose inner being has been oriented toward divine goodness (Psa 73:28) naturally overflows toward others (Rom 15:14: "full of goodness... able to instruct one another").
(2) Spirit-filling — the Spirit-produced goodness of 885-001 and 886-002 is the enabling source. Gal 5:22 presents chrēstotēs and agathōsunē as Spirit-fruit — the inner experience of Spirit-filling produces the capacity to express goodness toward others. Col 3:12 frames this as "putting on" kindness — a deliberate inner act enabled by the Spirit's prior work.
resolved_qa.question_text: What inner-being experience cultivates the capacity to extend the word to others?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 11:22: divine kindness as pole of bipolarity with severity; relational-conditional dynamic
resolved_qa.answer:
Three closing orientations are named:
(1) Self-direction — Isa 65:2: "a rebellious people who walk in a way that is not good, following their own devices." Following one's own devices is the inner orientation that closes off the good way.
(2) Refusal of the good way — Jer 6:16: explicitly named. The people are offered "the good way" and refuse it ("we will not walk in it"). The will's refusal is the closing mechanism.
(3) Moral self-justification in wrong orientation — Jer 44:17 (884-006 notes): the people recall false prosperity in idolatry as "good" — the inner orientation that redefines what is good according to its own advantage closes off recognition of genuine goodness.
(4) Unbelief / departure — Rom 11:22 (OBS-043): "otherwise you too will be cut off" — departure from faith/trust is named as the condition that closes off the ongoing experience of divine kindness.
resolved_qa.question_text: What inner-being orientation closes off the reception of the word?

resolved_qa.finding: Eze 36:31: not-good verdict internalised → self-loathing; affective register as significant as cognitive
resolved_qa.answer:
Both are present and distinguishable:
(1) Orientation change — the primary transformation in the experiential group (884-003) is reorientation: Psa 73:28 shows Asaph's inner life transformed not by changed external circumstances but by a reorientation toward God. "It is good to be near God" — the transformation is in where the person now locates goodness.
(2) Character transformation — the Spirit-produced goodness (885-001, 886-002) is a transformation of the person's inner character. The Spirit fills the person with goodness; Col 3:12 images this as putting on a new garment — the character itself changes.
(3) Moral awareness transformation — Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the transformation from moral blindness to moral self-recognition is an inner condition change — the person who did not see their deeds as not-good comes to see and loathe them. This is a change of condition (from blindness to sight) that precedes orientation change (loathing → turning).
resolved_qa.question_text: What kind of inner-being transformation does the word produce — does it change the condition or the person's orientation to the condition?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 73:28: experiential good = nearness to God as highest inner-being state
resolved_qa.answer:
Goodness produces a particular quality of inner stability characterised by rootedness in God rather than in circumstances:
- Psa 73:28 (OBS-029): Asaph's inner stability after his crisis of doubt is entirely grounded in nearness to God — "it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge." The stability is relational and divine-oriented, not circumstantial.
- The doxological group (884-001): the declaration "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1, 107:1) names an enduring quality — God's goodness as the stable reference point regardless of circumstances. The human person's inner stability is anchored in this enduring divine character.
- Contrasted with Haman (Est 5:9, OBS-040): the inner well-being not grounded in God is instantly disrupted — its instability marks the absence of genuine rootedness.
The quality of stability produced by goodness is: oriented toward God, grounded in his enduring character, resistant to circumstantial disruption.
resolved_qa.question_text: What quality of inner-being stability does the word produce?

resolved_qa.finding: Est 5:9: glad-of-heart (Haman) — inner well-being fragile and morally ungrounded
resolved_qa.answer:
Goodness produces a particular quality of inner stability characterised by rootedness in God rather than in circumstances:
- Psa 73:28 (OBS-029): Asaph's inner stability after his crisis of doubt is entirely grounded in nearness to God — "it is good to be near God; I have made the Lord God my refuge." The stability is relational and divine-oriented, not circumstantial.
- The doxological group (884-001): the declaration "for he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1, 107:1) names an enduring quality — God's goodness as the stable reference point regardless of circumstances. The human person's inner stability is anchored in this enduring divine character.
- Contrasted with Haman (Est 5:9, OBS-040): the inner well-being not grounded in God is instantly disrupted — its instability marks the absence of genuine rootedness.
The quality of stability produced by goodness is: oriented toward God, grounded in his enduring character, resistant to circumstantial disruption.
resolved_qa.question_text: What quality of inner-being stability does the word produce?

resolved_qa.finding: G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity" — semantic overlap with R65 noted
resolved_qa.answer:
Three capacities are named:
(1) Instruction — Rom 15:14: "full of goodness, filled with all knowledge, and able to instruct one another." Goodness produces the capacity to teach and build up others in the community.
(2) Kindness as active relational quality — Col 3:12: chrēstotēs as garment of the renewed person enables generous relational disposition toward others. The capacity is dispositional — not occasional kindness but a settled orientation.
(3) Justice and right action — Mic 6:8: the person who knows what is good has the capacity to do justice toward others and to love kindness in relational practice.
(4) Beneficence/generosity — agathōsunē glossed as "generosity" in Gal 5:22 (OBS-008): the capacity to act generously toward others as an expression of Spirit-produced goodness.
resolved_qa.question_text: What capacity toward others does the word produce in the inner being of the one who has received it?

resolved_qa.finding: Eze 36:31: not-good verdict internalised → self-loathing; affective register as significant as cognitive
resolved_qa.answer:
The most direct evidence: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) — "all have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one." The inner-being condition of the person without goodness is: turned aside (direction reversed), worthless (character emptied), unable to do good (capacity absent). This is the universal-depravity diagnosis — total inner-being failure at the goodness level.
Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) describes the prior condition of those who have not yet received the transforming recognition: walking in evil ways, doing deeds that are not good, under iniquity and abomination — the condition is morally blind, self-directed, unaware of the verdict on their ways.
Jer 6:16 names the condition of those who refuse the good way as one of deliberate refusal: the way is offered and rejected. This is not ignorance but wilful resistance.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word reveal about the inner-being condition of the person who does not receive it?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 3:12: chrēstotēs by absence = diagnosis of fallen inner being; catena context gives force
resolved_qa.answer:
The most direct evidence: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) — "all have turned aside; together they have become worthless; no one does good, not even one." The inner-being condition of the person without goodness is: turned aside (direction reversed), worthless (character emptied), unable to do good (capacity absent). This is the universal-depravity diagnosis — total inner-being failure at the goodness level.
Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) describes the prior condition of those who have not yet received the transforming recognition: walking in evil ways, doing deeds that are not good, under iniquity and abomination — the condition is morally blind, self-directed, unaware of the verdict on their ways.
Jer 6:16 names the condition of those who refuse the good way as one of deliberate refusal: the way is offered and rejected. This is not ignorance but wilful resistance.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the word reveal about the inner-being condition of the person who does not receive it?

resolved_qa.finding: Registry description names 3 axes: aesthetic/moral/relational; human goodness is derivative
resolved_qa.answer: Mic 6:8 implies the relationship without stating it explicitly: God tells the human what is good — the knowledge of goodness flows from God to the human. The registry description (OBS-001) makes the relationship explicit: human goodness is derivative — a participation in and reflection of divine goodness. Psa 119:68 is more direct — "You are good and do good; teach me your statutes" — the human learns goodness from the God who is good. The relationship is: divine goodness is originative and instructive; human goodness is responsive and dependent.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the primary verse identify as the relationship between the human version and the divine version of the word?

resolved_qa.finding: 885-001 Volition (04) — straddles character and agency; assignment plausible not obvious
resolved_qa.answer: Per Q&A-016 (OBS-016 equivalent). Three directions: (1) Divine → human: God's goodness flows toward the human person through character, word, creation, and Spirit. (2) Inward → outward: goodness received in the inner being expresses itself outward in conduct, community, and testimony. (3) Vertical → horizontal: the vertical orientation (walking with God, nearness to God) produces horizontal expression (justice, kindness toward others). The directional structure is: downward from God → inward to the human person → outward to community.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the verse evidence reveal about the direction of the word's operation?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 34:8: divine goodness as experiential encounter; shared anchor with discernment and hope
resolved_qa.answer: Two connections are present: (1) Lam 3:25–27 (OBS in Q&A-035): in the context of profound suffering (Lamentations), the declaration that "the Lord is good to those who wait for him" situates divine goodness as sustaining and accessible in affliction. Suffering does not negate goodness — it becomes the context in which the goodness of waiting for God is discovered. (2) Psa 34:8 (OBS-025): Psalm 34 is composed in the context of David's distress. The invitation to "taste and see that the Lord is good" is issued from within a crisis. Goodness and affliction are not opposed — affliction is a context in which divine goodness is specifically offered.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to suffering or affliction?

resolved_qa.finding: Eze 36:31: not-good verdict internalised → self-loathing; affective register as significant as cognitive
resolved_qa.answer: This is one of the richest connections in the registry. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) is the primary evidence: the recognition of deeds as not-good produces self-loathing before God — moral failure is the catalyst for the deepest moral awareness. Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) names the universal absence of goodness as the diagnosis of the fallen human condition. Group 884-006 (22 verses) is entirely dedicated to the not-good verdict — moral failure named and assessed. Goodness and moral failure are structurally related: goodness is understood partly through its absence in moral failure; and the recognition of moral failure is the first movement toward goodness. The shared anchor at Eze 36:31 with Registry 1 (abomination) confirms this connection.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to moral failure or guilt?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 3:12: chrēstotēs by absence = diagnosis of fallen inner being; catena context gives force
resolved_qa.answer: This is one of the richest connections in the registry. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035) is the primary evidence: the recognition of deeds as not-good produces self-loathing before God — moral failure is the catalyst for the deepest moral awareness. Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) names the universal absence of goodness as the diagnosis of the fallen human condition. Group 884-006 (22 verses) is entirely dedicated to the not-good verdict — moral failure named and assessed. Goodness and moral failure are structurally related: goodness is understood partly through its absence in moral failure; and the recognition of moral failure is the first movement toward goodness. The shared anchor at Eze 36:31 with Registry 1 (abomination) confirms this connection.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to moral failure or guilt?

resolved_qa.finding: Jos 23:14: inner-being reception of covenantal good — heart and soul as site of knowing
resolved_qa.answer: Group 884-005 ("God's good word and promise — covenantal faithfulness") is the primary evidence. Jos 23:14 (OBS-033): God's good promises are received and known in the "hearts and souls" of the people — covenantal faithfulness is internalised. The repeated formula in Ezra and Nehemiah ("the good hand of my God was upon me" — Ezr 7:9, Neh 2:8) names divine goodness as the operative power behind covenantal provision. God's good word is inherently covenantal — it is the promise made and kept within relationship. The doxological refrain "he is good, his steadfast love endures forever" (Psa 100:5, 106:1 etc.) links goodness directly to hesed (steadfast love) — the primary covenantal disposition of God.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to covenantal relationship?

resolved_qa.finding: Registry description names 3 axes: aesthetic/moral/relational; human goodness is derivative
resolved_qa.answer: Reception — consistently. Col 3:12: identity is established by prior reception — "God's chosen ones, holy and beloved" — this is their standing before the commission follows. Gal 5:22: the Spirit produces the fruit; the believer receives it. The good person in 884-002 is assessed by the quality of character (Pro 12:2 — "a good man obtains favour from the Lord") — but this character is itself a participation in divine goodness (OBS-001), not autonomous achievement. The ground of identity in this registry is reception and relationship, not self-achieved virtue.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does a key verse identify as the ground of the speaker's or recipient's identity — achievement, reception, or something else?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 11:22: divine kindness as pole of bipolarity with severity; relational-conditional dynamic
resolved_qa.answer: Deu 9:6 is the primary evidence: "not because of your righteousness, for you are a stubborn people" — the explicit negation of any moral basis. The good land and God's goodness are given despite (not because of) the people's character. Rom 11:22 (OBS-043) presents the condition differently — the recipient has no claim on divine kindness that is independent of God's character; the conditionality named is about continuation, not origination. The absence of any basis is more explicit in the Deuteronomy context than in the NT material for this registry.
resolved_qa.question_text: What structural condition in a key verse establishes the recipient as having no basis for the word they receive?

resolved_qa.finding: Eze 36:31: not-good verdict internalised → self-loathing; affective register as significant as cognitive
resolved_qa.answer: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) is the primary evidence for universal absence: "no one does good, not even one" — the complete withdrawal/absence of chrēstotēs from fallen humanity reveals that goodness is not a default human capacity but something that requires a source outside the natural human condition. Its absence defines what fallen humanity is. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the recognition of deeds as not-good reveals that goodness has a structural role as the evaluative standard — its absence is measured against its presence as the norm. Jer 6:16: the absence of goodness-walking produces restlessness and judgment. The absence reveals goodness as constitutive of human flourishing and covenantal standing.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does a verse about the withdrawal or absence of the word reveal about the word's role?

resolved_qa.finding: Rom 3:12: chrēstotēs by absence = diagnosis of fallen inner being; catena context gives force
resolved_qa.answer: Rom 3:12 (OBS-046) is the primary evidence for universal absence: "no one does good, not even one" — the complete withdrawal/absence of chrēstotēs from fallen humanity reveals that goodness is not a default human capacity but something that requires a source outside the natural human condition. Its absence defines what fallen humanity is. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): the recognition of deeds as not-good reveals that goodness has a structural role as the evaluative standard — its absence is measured against its presence as the norm. Jer 6:16: the absence of goodness-walking produces restlessness and judgment. The absence reveals goodness as constitutive of human flourishing and covenantal standing.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does a verse about the withdrawal or absence of the word reveal about the word's role?

resolved_qa.finding: Jer 26:14: volitional-preference anchor is Jeremiah's willed submission under threat — weighty instance
resolved_qa.answer: Jer 26:14 (Group 884-008 anchor, OBS-038): Jeremiah's "do with me as seems good and right to you" spoken under threat of death — this is the volitional-preference idiom in its most extreme form. The depth revealed: genuine goodness-orientation involves the capacity to yield one's own life to another's judgment. The inner depth is one of radical volitional submission. Lam 3:25–27 in extremity (national devastation, personal grief): "it is good to wait quietly for the salvation of the Lord" — goodness at its deepest is the capacity to orient toward God even in the absence of all external support.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does a verse about the word in extremity reveal about its depth of operation?

resolved_qa.finding: Eze 36:31: not-good verdict internalised → self-loathing; affective register as significant as cognitive
resolved_qa.answer: Group 884-006 (not-good verdict) provides the primary judicial evidence. Eze 36:31 (OBS-035): goodness functions as the standard against which conduct is judicially assessed — deeds are measured as not-good against the norm. Eze 18:18: "he did what is not good among his people, behold he shall die for his iniquity" — the not-good verdict carries judicial consequence (death). In the judicial context, goodness functions as the standard of moral evaluation — it defines what passes and what fails the test.
resolved_qa.question_text: What does a verse about the word in the judicial or accountability context reveal about its function?

resolved_qa.finding: G0019 Gal 5:22 glossed as "generosity" — semantic overlap with R65 noted
resolved_qa.answer:
Two Greek OWNER terms compete for primacy. G0019 (agathōsunē — goodness) is the more directly named ("goodness") but has only 4 verses. G5544 (chrēstotēs — kindness) has 7 verses across 2 groups. G0019's range: goodness, virtue, beneficence, generosity — it spans inner character (virtue), outward benefit (beneficence), and practical generosity. G5544's range: kindness, goodness, gentleness — it spans relational disposition (kindness), inner quality (goodness), and the divine attribute (generous goodwill). Both terms reveal that Greek goodness vocabulary is more specialised than H2896A — each term occupies a narrower semantic range, producing the NT's precision about the specific form goodness takes.
Source: Section D · OBS-008, OBS-009
resolved_qa.question_text: What is the primary Greek term, and what does its range of use reveal?

resolved_qa.finding: G5544 meaning parse includes Rom 3:12 negation — lexically confirms presence/absence range
resolved_qa.answer:
Two Greek OWNER terms compete for primacy. G0019 (agathōsunē — goodness) is the more directly named ("goodness") but has only 4 verses. G5544 (chrēstotēs — kindness) has 7 verses across 2 groups. G0019's range: goodness, virtue, beneficence, generosity — it spans inner character (virtue), outward benefit (beneficence), and practical generosity. G5544's range: kindness, goodness, gentleness — it spans relational disposition (kindness), inner quality (goodness), and the divine attribute (generous goodwill). Both terms reveal that Greek goodness vocabulary is more specialised than H2896A — each term occupies a narrower semantic range, producing the NT's precision about the specific form goodness takes.
Source: Section D · OBS-008, OBS-009
resolved_qa.question_text: What is the primary Greek term, and what does its range of use reveal?

resolved_qa.finding: G5544 meaning parse includes Rom 3:12 negation — lexically confirms presence/absence range
resolved_qa.answer:
The LXX connection is not directly addressed in the Stage 2a observations. What can be inferred: G5544 (chrēstotēs) in its meaning parse (Section D) references Rom 3:12 which is a quotation from Ps 14:3 LXX — confirming that chrēstotēs was used in the LXX to render a Hebrew moral-quality term. The related words G5541 (chrēsteuomai — be kind) and G5543 (chrēstos — good/kind) suggest a LXX-rooted word family that the NT inherits. For H2896A, the LXX rendering as agathos/kalos provides the lexical bridge to NT usage. This is noted as thin evidence — LSJ data was absent for G0019 and G5544 (Section D notes).
Source: Section D · OBS-009
resolved_qa.question_text: What does the LXX use of the vocabulary reveal about the continuity or development of the word across the Testaments?

resolved_qa.finding: G5544 meaning parse includes Rom 3:12 negation — lexically confirms presence/absence range
resolved_qa.answer:
G5544 (chrēstotēs) may carry this. In classical Greek, chrēstos (related form) referred broadly to "useful, serviceable, good" — applicable to persons, tools, or conditions. In the NT, chrēstotēs is spiritually and pneumatologically specialised: it becomes a Spirit-fruit (Gal 5:22) and a divine attribute (Rom 11:22, Eph 2:7). The direction shifts from horizontal utility (what is serviceable) to vertical character (what the Spirit produces and what God embodies). This is not a reversal of direction so much as a concentration and elevation of meaning. LSJ data absent — this observation is partially supported only.
Source: Section D · OBS-009
resolved_qa.question_text: Does any term reveal a classical-to-NT directional reversal — a term whose direction of operation changes between its classical use and its NT use?

resolved_qa.finding: Cognition (03) assigned to 884-004 and 884-006 — both evaluative judgment groups
resolved_qa.answer:
Confirmed dimensions in R67 (from Section F): Moral Character (05) — 4 groups; Cognition (03) — 2 groups; Volition (04) — 1 group; Divine-Human Correspondence (11) — 2 groups; NULL — 3 groups. Each of these dimensions is almost certainly shared with adjacent characteristics — Moral Character (05) is likely the most common dimension across C10 and related clusters. Whether any single cluster shares all confirmed dimensions simultaneously is not determinable from Stage 2a data alone — this requires cross-cluster analysis (Session D territory).
Source: Section F · OBS-012
resolved_qa.question_text: How many of this word's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another cluster or characteristic — and is there any cluster that shares all confirmed dimensions?

resolved_qa.finding: Psa 34:8: divine goodness as experiential encounter; shared anchor with discernment and hope
resolved_qa.answer:
The shared anchor at Psa 34:8 with Registry 59 (faith — per Section G.3) is the structural connection point. Faith and goodness are structurally related at the moment of encounter: "taste and see that the Lord is good; blessed is the man who takes refuge in him" — the act of taking refuge is a faith-act produced by the encounter with divine goodness. Goodness is thus prior to faith in sequence (encounter with goodness → faith response); faith is the inner-being response to tasting goodness. The distinction: goodness is the character attribute encountered; faith is the inner-being response to the encounter. 2Th 1:11 connects them from the other direction: God completes "every resolve for good and every work of faith" — goodness and faith are co-products of divine enabling, parallel expressions.
Source: Section G.3, Section J · OBS-025
resolved_qa.question_text: How does the study distinguish this word from faith — and what is their structural relationship?
